channel.title: AP News 全文 RSS（简体中文版）
channel.description: AP News 首页抓取 + 英译简

item.title: 万斯和伊朗官员正在瑞士就德黑兰核计划进行会谈
item.description:
OBBUERGEN, Switzerland (AP) — U.S. Vice PresidentJD Vanceand senior Iranian officials are set to formally launch negotiations on Sunday overTehran’s nuclear program, building out thefragile interim deal to end the warin Iran and keeping theStrait of Hormuzopen.
The framework was signed last week, and now top American and Iranian negotiators are in a60-day sprint to reach an agreementon the technical details that hold massive implications for the world economy and global security.
Iran dealt two quick blows to the interim agreement with the United States on Saturday, angered by Israel’s continued attacks in Lebanon, saying it had closed the Strait of Hormuz and announcing that while its negotiators were going to Switzerland for talks, not much is likely to happen there.
Yet only days after signing the agreement, it is being stress-tested after fighting escalated in Lebanon between Israel and the Iranian-backed militant group Hezbollah — and by thesubsequent announcement by Iran’s militarythat it had again closed the vital waterway that transits one-fifth of the world’s traded oil and natural gas. A renewed ceasefire in Lebanon, brokered on Saturday, appeared to be holding up.
“The question before us now is how much more can we accomplish together? Can we turn over a new leaf?” Vance said in brief comments as the talks, dubbed the “Lake Lucerne Summit,” got underway. “Can we change relations in the Middle East permanently, or do we go back to doing things the old way, which is not our preference, but is certainly very much something that can happen.”
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi briefly appeared in the same room as Vance before the U.S. vice president offered his remarks in front of the media.
Vance first sat down for talks with Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Staff Field Marshall Asim Munir, who has served as a key intermediary between Washington and Tehran throughout the conflict.
U.S. Vice President JD Vance, right, meets with Pakistan’s Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, during high-level talks aimed at advancing a deal to end the Middle East conflict, at the Bürgenstock Resort in Obbuergen, near Lucerne, in Switzerland, Sunday, June 21, 2026. (Nathan Howard/Pool Photo via AP)
“What’s up, man! Good to see you,” Vance said as he warmly greeted Munir, who serves as Pakistan’s army chief.
Sharif met separately with Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, who is leading Tehran’s delegation, and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi.
Mediators from Qatar were also on hand at the mountainside resort near Lake Lucerne.
Rafael Grossi, chief of the U.N. nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency, met with Swiss Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis on the sidelines of the gathering. The agency had monitored the 2015 nuclear deal negotiated between the U.S. and Iran under the Democratic Obama administration. Trump, a Republican, withdrew the U.S. from the agreement in 2018.
Iran’s main focus during the negotiations will be the ongoing war between Israel and Lebanon, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei told Iran’s state news agency.
Iran is insisting that the deal’s implementation start with the part of the deal that calls for a cessation of all wars, including between Israel and Hezbollah. Baghaei said the U.S. “has been unable or unwilling” to holdIsrael to the ceasefire.
Iranian officials were to hold their own meetings with Pakistani and Qatari mediators before a planned four-way meeting that would include the U.S. negotiating team.
Iran is cautiously approaching the negotiations given its previous experience with the U.S. negotiations on the nuclear issue, which twice in the past year have been interrupted by massive strikes against the country. “The implementation of any document is more important than its signing,” Baghaei said Sunday.
But Iran’s president added that Iran will maintain its right to a nuclear program.
“What is certain is that we will never back down from the right to enrich uranium, and the other side is also forced to accept it,” Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said on Sunday, according to Iran’s state media.
Vance had originally been slated to be on the ground at the Bürgenstock resort near Lucerne on Friday, buthis departure from the United States was delayedafter fighting escalated in Lebanon and Iranian officials canceled plans to attend the talks.
U.S. Central Command disputed Iran’s claim that it had once again shuttered the strait and said U.S. forces continued to monitor the situation to ensure traffic continues to flow through the waterway. Vance has said thatmillions of barrels of oil have moved through the straitin recent days.
Vance departed the U.S. just after Iranian state TV said Iran’s negotiators had arrived in Switzerland.
The vice president was joined by special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, President Donald Trump’s son-in-law, for Sunday’s talks. Witkoff and Kushner were on the ground in Switzerland ahead of Vance to begin sifting through the technical details of the nuclear talks.
Vance and his wife, second lady Usha Vance, arrived at Emmen Air Base outside Lucerne just before 6 a.m. local time, according to his office.
While Vance said he planned to be in Switzerland for just “a day or two,” leaving much of the detailed negotiations to be spearheaded by Witkoff and Kushner, his role in the talks hasheightened scrutiny of the vice presidentat a time when he’s actively considering a 2028 presidential campaign.
Trump and Vance have come undersearing criticism from parts of their own partyfor the deal, with Republican hard-liners unfavorably likening it to a nuclear agreement signed by the Obama administration that Trump and the GOP have insisted did nothing to actually terminate Iran’s nuclear program.
Theagreement signed by Trump and Iranian President Pezeshkianimmediately allows Tehran to sell its oil freely and paves the way for Iran to tap into billions of dollars in assets that are currently frozen. It also calls for Iran to dilute its stockpile of highly enriched uranium, believed to be buried under nuclear sites that were targeted in U.S. strikes last summer.
The agreement says commercial vessels can pass through the Strait of Hormuz for 60 days without a charge, but does not preclude future fees imposed by Iran. Trump made his own threat on Saturday to levy U.S. tolls on the strait if there is no deal with Iran in 60 days, insisting in a social media post that the money would be for “services rendered as the Guardian Angel to the countries of the Middle East.”
The Trump administration has been working to reassure global markets that the Iran war has been merely a blip on oil prices, as Americans have complained the conflict resulted in hiking gasoline prices ahead of peak summer travel months. After the White House announced the deal a week ago, oil futures dropped almost 8% — and markets are expected to closely track the progress of talks when they open for trading on Sunday evening.
Further complicating matters, neither Israel nor Hezbollah is a signatory to the deal between the U.S. and Iran, and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has vowed to keep his forces in southern Lebanon until any threat to Israel is eliminated. Hezbollah has refused to halt its attacks unless Israel commits to withdrawing from Lebanon.
Fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in the initial days after the agreement between the U.S. and Iran killed 47 people in Lebanon, as well as five Israeli soldiers.
Kim reported from Washington. Associated Press writers Munir Ahmed in Islamabad, Amir Vahdat in Tehran, Iran, and Melanie Lidman in Tel Aviv, Israel, contributed to this report.

item.title: 玻利维亚总统宣布进入紧急状态，道路封锁导致供应中断
item.description:
玻利维亚拉巴斯（美联社）——总统罗德里戈·帕松周六宣布进入紧急状态，赋予军方广泛权力，以解除阻碍玻利维亚政府所在地和其他主要城市燃料和食品供应的道路封锁。
过去五周发生的一波抗议活动要求帕斯因政府实施的紧缩措施（包括取消燃料补贴和其他问题）下台。据当局称，示威活动引发了持炸药的示威者与防暴警察之间的暴力对抗，导致至少 365 人被捕、37 人受伤。
据玻利维亚监察员办公室和人权组织称，至少有 17 人死亡，其中大多数与交通中断导致缺乏医疗服务有关。
政府表示，在主要道路上设置的路障实际上隔离了拉巴斯市，引发燃料和食品短缺，交通瘫痪，并阻止患者前往医院，导致至少七人因缺乏医疗护理而死亡。
总统在向全国发表的电视讲话中表示：“这不是限制人们生命的紧急状态。这是让人们重获自由的紧急状态。”
随着抗议活动期间企业关门、超市货架空空如也、医院氧气耗尽，社会某些阶层呼吁帕斯通过武力恢复秩序的呼声不断升级。
周五晚上，帕斯与一个工会签署了一项协议，工会领导人呼吁解除封锁。但其他抗议者要求帕斯辞职并拒绝谈判。
帕兹表示，紧急状态的目的是保证燃料供应，由于路障导致油罐车滞留，燃料供应变得越来越稀缺。
该法令禁止“以影响交通和供应的方式封锁街道、大道、道路和高速公路”，并命令武装部队暂时支持警察“恢复秩序、重新开放道路和保护民众”。根据该法令，紧急状态并不限制正当程序权利或宪法保障，并允许人们继续进行日常活动。
政府在一份声明中表示，紧急状态将持续 90 天，但如果“针对民众的暴力和威胁结束”，紧急状态可能会提前解除。
帕斯于 11 月上台，结束了玻利维亚社会主义运动党 (MAS) 近 20 年不间断的统治，该党引发了该国一代人以来最严重的经济危机。帕兹是一位战胜了较为保守的候选人的中间派，他承诺解决长期的燃料短缺问题，补充央行几乎空空如也的金库，同时保护代表金管局受欢迎程度支柱的社会福利。
但他的紧缩措施，最重要的是取消长期的燃料补贴，加剧了通货膨胀。他的政府解决了燃料短缺问题，但劣质汽油损坏了数千辆汽车。鼓励外国投资和刺激经济增长的改革在国会陷入停滞。
高地原住民和农村工人团体领导了抗议活动，指责他的政府自上任以来忽视了他们的需求。他们长期支持“争取社会主义运动”，但去年帮助帕斯上台。
他面临着来自玻利维亚国会的极右翼和长期执政的左翼不断增加的压力。前总统埃沃·莫拉莱斯支持抗议活动，并要求在他位于古柯种植热带地区的藏身处举行新的选举，他在那里逃避因法定强奸罪的逮捕令。
特朗普政府支持帕斯，在莫拉莱斯领导下的玻利维亚多年来反西方敌对之后，帕斯修复了与美国的关系。
美国国务卿马可·卢比奥上周致电帕斯，告知他华盛顿正在“加大紧急援助和后勤业务支持”，以帮助缓解封锁造成的短缺。
美国国防部长皮特·赫格斯谴责抗议活动是“企图推翻合法政府”，并向那些他所说的“从我们西半球的死亡和破坏中获利”的人发出严厉警告。
“美国正在关注，”他在 X 上写道。
关注美联社对拉丁美洲和加勒比地区的报道：https://apnews.com/hub/latin-america

item.title: 西班牙法官命令首相夫人接受腐败审判并交出护照
item.description:
西班牙巴塞罗那（美联社）——周六，一名法官下令西班牙首相佩德罗·桑切斯托的妻子因涉嫌以权谋私和腐败受审，并交出护照。
调查法官胡安·卡洛斯·佩纳多 (Juan Carlos Peinado) 做出了裁决，认为贝戈尼亚·戈麦斯存在潜逃风险。除了交出护照外，她还必须每两周出庭一次。审判日期尚未确定。
这一决定引发了激烈的政治对抗，反对派呼吁桑切斯的社会党政府辞职。
戈麦斯被指控利用自己的职位影响政府向一批科技公司提供的合同。法官还指控她在聘请顾问时滥用公共资金，以及在公立大学担任教授时不当使用软件。
戈麦斯否认有任何不当行为。桑切斯称，此案是保守派政治对手发起的抹黑运动的一部分，目的是推翻自 2018 年以来一直执政的左翼政府。
佩纳多表示，一名据称从政府合同中受益的商人和为戈麦斯工作的顾问也将接受审判。
Spain’s Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez talks to journalists as he arrives for the EU summit in Brussels, Thursday, June 18, 2026. (AP Photo/Marius Burgelman)
桑切斯是美国总统唐纳德·特朗普的批评者，在明年大选之前面临多方面的法律麻烦。
本周早些时候，前社会党总理何塞·路易斯·罗德里格斯·萨帕特罗 (José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero) 因涉嫌在政府航空公司救助中所扮演的角色而出现在另一位法官面前，并解释了警方突袭其办公室时发现的高端珠宝的情况。他否认有不当行为。
政府官员严厉批评周六的决定是出于政治动机，而社会党则称其为“绝对的民主丑闻”。
该党表示：​​“贝戈尼亚·戈麦斯是无辜的。”“两年来，她一直是政治和司法迫害的目标。今天的事态发展只是最新的升级。”
但西班牙保守反对派继续发起攻击，敦促政府提前举行选举。
“Lawmakers and the architects of our constitution could never have imagined that the threats to our democracy could originate from the Spanish government itself,” said Miguel Tellado, secretary-general of the main opposition People’s Party.
“现在我们看到政府如何攻击法官、检察官和媒体，同时试图压制反对党，”他说。“这在任何现代民主国家都是不可想象的。”
The two-year investigation into Gómez was launched following accusations by the pressure group Manos Limpias, or Clean Hands, which has pursued multiple legal cases, many linked to conservative causes.
德里克·加托普洛斯 (Derek Gatopoulos) 在希腊雅典为本报告做出了贡献。

item.title: 3名徒步旅行者因高温事故死亡后，大峡谷预计将再次出现极端高温
item.description:
亚利桑那州大峡谷国家公园（美联社）——最近内峡谷内与高温相关的事件增加，其中包括三名徒步旅行者死亡，大峡谷国家公园的游客收到警告，下周初将出现极端气温。
美国国家气象局发布了周一至周二中午大峡谷的极端高温警报，预测低海拔幻影牧场 (Phantom Ranch) 的气温可能达到或超过 110 华氏度（43 摄氏度）。
国家公园管理局在本周发布的一份声明中表示，“强烈建议”人们避免在中午徒步旅行，原因是“最近发生了一系列与高温相关的事件”。
6 月 16 日，极端高温警报生效，两名分别为 67 岁和 68 岁的徒步旅行者被发现死在北凯巴布步道上，NPS 称这条步道是主要内峡谷步道中最困难的一条。该部门表示，他们似乎已经死于与高温有关的疾病症状。
NPS 表示，第三名 72 岁的人于 6 月 12 日在南凯巴布步道 (South Kaibab Trail) 因中暑而死亡。
周五晚间，在以南约 90 英里（145 公里）处，橡树溪峡谷 (Oak Creek Canyon) 的游客和居民被疏散，因为野火烧毁了塞多纳 (Sedona) 以北数百英亩的土地。
从落基山脉到太平洋海岸的美国西部大部分地区周六气温高于平均水平，预计下周初天气会更加炎热。官员还警告说，长期干燥炎热的天气和相对较低的湿度增加了火灾危险。
公园和气象官员都向游客强调，徒步旅行的条件可能是骗人的。大峡谷边缘的温度通常比徒步旅行者在谷底的温度低 20 至 25 度。
“这只是大峡谷底部的一个炎热地方，”国家气象局弗拉格斯塔夫办公室的气象学家贾斯汀约翰德罗说。约翰德罗警告说，该地区正接近一年中最热的时期，随后夏季晚些时候的雨季将带来一些缓解。
徒步旅行者可能会遇到凉爽的气温，更容易下山开始下山路线，但他们要面临数千英尺的激烈攀登，并且要返回时，峡谷底部的温度要高得多。这些情况可能会导致游客出现中暑症状。
“即使是在温和的天气里，这也是非常费力的，”约翰德罗谈到返回边缘的徒步旅行时说道。“如果温度达到 105 到 110 度，就会导致一些非常严重的问题。”
西南地区综合事件管理团队的消防信息官员迪克·弗莱什曼 (Dick Fleishman) 表示，一个联邦跨部门团队和至少十几个地方机构正在努力扑灭大火，这场大火烧毁了橡树溪峡谷附近近 300 英亩（12 公顷）非常陡峭和崎岖的地形。
大火集中在塞多纳以北约7英里（11公里）处的红岩-秘密山荒野地区，但开始蔓延到可科尼诺国家森林。消防队员正在努力控制火势，防止火势向居民和游客被疏散的橡树溪峡谷或塞多纳蔓延，并为火势蔓延的可能性做好准备。
弗莱什曼说，陡峭的斜坡、附近的财产处于危险之中、火灾产生的热量以及火灾后雨水冲下斜坡造成洪水的风险是袖珍火灾特别令人担忧的原因之一。
“这场火灾的复杂性迅速加剧，”他说。“我们希望尽量减少占地面积。”
邻近国道约 30 英里（50 公里）双向封闭。
可科尼诺国家森林公园于周六下午正式关闭该地区所有露营地、野餐地点和步道起点。
“6 月 20 日，”驾车穿过该地区的弗莱什曼说道，“我从未见过如此安静的情况。”
橡树溪峡谷每年吸引数百万游客。

item.title: 芝加哥警方称，一辆SUV停下来向人群开枪后，至少有12人中枪
item.description:
芝加哥（美联社）——警方称，芝加哥街道上一辆SUV停下来，车内两人开始开枪，人群中至少有12人中枪。
警方在一份新闻稿中称，周五晚间枪击事件发生后，这辆SUV驶离南区社区，造成两人生命垂危。其中一人大腿受枪伤。
这群人中的八男四女年龄从 17 岁到 47 岁不等。他们正在四家医院接受治疗。
警方称，另一名男子伤势不明，并拒绝接受治疗。
警方最初接到有人中枪的报警，发现一名妇女背部有两处枪伤，一名男子背部有四处擦伤。两者均状况良好。
目前尚未立即获得更多信息。
警方报告称，自周五晚上以来，该市至少有 21 人遭到枪击，造成 4 人死亡。
枪击事件发生在六月十六日，这是一个庆祝美国奴隶制终结的节日。周五早些时候，前总统巴拉克·奥巴马和前第一夫人米歇尔·奥巴马欢迎第一批参观者来到他位于南区的总统中心。
当地枪支犯罪受害者权益倡导者多诺万·普莱斯牧师告诉哥伦比亚广播公司新闻，在假期看到这样的大规模枪击事件是一场悲剧。
“应该庆祝，”他说。“烟花不应该变成枪声。”

item.title: 新发布的视频记录了一名青少年在德克萨斯州田径运动会上被刺伤的后果
item.description:
德克萨斯州弗里斯科（美联社）——去年高中比赛期间，一名青少年运动员在体育场看台上刺死了另一名来自对手球队的青少年，德克萨斯州跑道上一名警员随身携带的摄像头和监控摄像头最新发布的视频捕捉到了这一瞬间。
6 月 10 日，19 岁的卡梅洛·安东尼因杀害 17 岁的奥斯汀·梅特卡夫而被判犯有谋杀罪，被判处 35 年监禁。陪审团驳回了安东尼的自卫主张。这些视频包含在科林县法院审判结束后发布的一批证据中。
监控视频显示雨天的跑道和看台。突然，一个穿着灰色运动衫的身影从黄色帐篷后面突然出现，然后跑下台阶。视频没有声音。
他到达看台底部，被绊倒在地上，然后沿着将看台和跑道分开的围栏边缘继续奔跑。他停了下来，扭头看了一眼似乎有人在追他，然后继续跑。
在绕着赛道走了一段路后，一名身份不明的人加入了他。他们停下来说话，然后拥抱在一起。他们又开始行走，另外一个人也加入了他们的行列。说了更多之后，安东尼走向栅栏，在那里他似乎遇到了一名警察。
警察给他戴上手铐，带他走向警车。安东尼听从了警官的命令，然后开始哭泣。
“他把手放在我身上，”安东尼用断断续续的声音说道。“我告诉他不要这样做。他把手放在我身上。”
警察护送他到警车上并将他安置在里面。

item.title: 特朗普加深了与意大利梅洛尼的争执，梅洛尼表示他的“无端攻击毫无意义”
item.description:
华盛顿（美联社）——周六，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普猛烈抨击意大利总理乔治亚·梅洛尼，坚称她在最近举行的七国集团峰会上“一遍又一遍”要求与他合影，并批评他所说的意大利在伊朗战争期间缺乏合作。
这些言论加深了本周因这位共和党总统接受一家意大利广播公司采访而开始的争执，特朗普在采访中声称梅洛尼在法国七国集团会议期间“乞求”了这张照片。梅洛尼称这“完全是捏造的”。由于梅洛尼政府为她辩护，这场争吵导致意大利外交部长取消了原定的美国之行。
2026 年 6 月 16 日星期二，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普（中）在法国埃维昂莱班与德国总理弗里德里希·梅尔茨（左）和意大利总理乔治亚·梅洛尼（右）共进工作午餐，与七国集团和中东领导人交谈。（伊芙琳·霍克斯坦/美联社泳池照片）
特朗普在戴维营总统度假胜地度过周末时在其社交媒体平台上写道：“在法国举行的七国集团会议期间，意大利总理吉乔治亚·梅洛尼一遍又一遍地要求与我合影。”他在最初的帖子中拼错了她的名字，后来他更正了。
他继续说道：“就她的受欢迎程度而言，她在意大利的表现不佳，可能是因为她在拒绝伊朗获得或发展核武器方面拒绝了美利坚合众国这个真正热爱和保护意大利的国家（但北约也是如此！）。”
梅洛尼很快做出回应，在给特朗普的一份声明中表示，“这些持续的、无端的攻击是毫无意义的。”
“至于我的受欢迎程度，成为你的朋友肯定没有帮助，也不取决于我和你的关系。我的受欢迎程度取决于我捍卫意大利国家利益的能力，而这正是我一直在做的，”梅洛尼在Instagram上发帖说。她补充道，“无论如何，我的受欢迎程度与你无关。我建议你专注于你的。”
两位强大领导人之间出现裂痕之际，特朗普与欧洲的关系长期以来一直处于紧张状态，主要是因为美国政府对欧洲大陆的贸易政策、总统持续威胁要控制格陵兰岛（丹麦的自治领土）以及特朗普决定打击伊朗。
在刚刚结束的法国埃维昂莱班七国集团峰会上，特朗普对联盟中的其他欧洲领导人采取了更为温和的语气，因为他们支持他结束伊朗战争的临时协议。但随着特朗普下个月前往土耳其参加北约年度峰会，预计紧张局势将再次全面显现。
特朗普的初步评论于周五在 La7 网络上播出。一名记者向总统询问有关乌克兰的问题，但特朗普提出了梅洛尼并就这张照片发表了声明。La7 表示，特朗普表示他没有义务与她合影，但他为她感到难过并同意了。广播公司在网上发布了对话的配音版本，但不是原始的英语音频。
2026 年 6 月 16 日星期二在法国埃维昂莱班举行的七国集团峰会期间，七国集团领导人和受邀国家合影后，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普（左）与意大利总理乔治亚·梅洛尼（左二）进行了交谈。（美联社照片/Thibault Camus）
特朗普在帖子中还抱怨梅洛尼不允许美国在伊朗战争期间使用意大利的跑道或跑道，尽管美国是北约盟国中国防开支领先的国家。这是对军事联盟的长期抱怨，也是特朗普在周三与北约秘书长马克·鲁特举行白宫会议以及在土耳其安卡拉举行的北约峰会之前再次提出的抱怨。
意大利是美国的重要后勤枢纽，今年三月，未经议会批准，意大利拒绝允许前往中东的美国轰炸机使用西西里岛的基地。这一决定反映了宪法的限制和国内对战争的强烈反对。梅洛尼坚称，任何利用意大利基地进行进攻行动的行为都需要议会的支持。
特朗普周六表达了他对梅洛尼德的不满，声称鉴于美国和伊朗之间达成的结束战争的初步协议，她“想再次成为朋友”。
美联社驻罗马撰稿人西尔维娅·斯特拉奇对此报道做出了贡献。

item.title: 特朗普试图将倒影池的问题归咎于破坏行为，但没有提供证据
item.description:
华盛顿（美联社）——周六，唐纳德·特朗普总统宣布，联邦当局“逮捕了多名”他所说的破坏倒影池的人，同时他很难解释为什么他为建国 250 周年而启动的耗资 1400 万多美元的修复项目似乎适得其反。
唐纳德·特朗普总统的改造项目旨在将颜色变成“美国国旗蓝色”，几天后，藻类大量繁殖使林肯纪念堂倒影池变成了绿色。（美联社视频：内森·埃尔格伦）
特朗普表示，他的前任让泳池变成了被藻类污染的绿色，他将在泳池上标上“美国国旗蓝色”，这样可以更好地反映华盛顿纪念碑。但新泳池揭幕后，它的蓝色很快变成了熟悉的绿色。工人们用化学物质处理它以杀死藻类，但随后底部涂成的蓝色衬里开始剥落。
周五晚上，特朗普发布了有关游泳池的信息。
2026 年 6 月 20 日星期六，华盛顿，游客观看国家公园管理局员工使用吸尘器清洁林肯纪念堂倒影池。（美联社照片/马克·希费尔贝因）
“我们在美丽的倒影池遇到了一些真正的破坏问题，”他周五晚上在社交媒体网站上发帖说。“就像三天前一样，他们毁掉了泳池外面的草，还千方百计地伤害了刚刚安装好的内表面。”
他没有提供任何细节来证实他的说法。
负责国家广场执法和维护的机构——美国公园警察、国家公园管理局和内政部——没有回应置评请求。特朗普周六随后发帖称，公园警察“逮捕了多名破坏我们国家宏伟的反映民意调查的人”，随后将他的拼写纠正为“Pool”。
他接着说：“谁会做这样的事？这些都是与破坏国家古迹有关的非常严重的罪行。入狱多年！”
特朗普后来在一篇帖子中承认，倒影池需要再次修复，以使其恢复到与以前“同等的美丽水平”。他写道：“我们今天会见了承包商，他们可能会被迫释放和排出大部分水，以便进行必要的维修，但会尽快完成。”
被捕的男子是马里兰州贝塞斯达 67 岁的戴维·赫恩 (David Hearn)，他拥有一家生产用于建造船只的复合材料的公司。他说，周五，他在 64 英里的自行车骑行过程中在泳池边停下来看看发生了什么事。
前奥运会皮划艇运动员赫恩告诉美联社，他把手伸进泳池，因为他想检查剥落的新涂层。他说，他短暂地触摸了仍然附着在泳池一侧的一块块，然后在公园工作人员告诉他后不久就放开了。
但是，赫恩说，他随后被国民警卫队和公园警察拘留了五个小时，然后于周五晚上被释放。
“我是一个好奇的公民，”赫恩在电话采访中说。“我伸手想看看它是什么感觉。它非常有弹性。”
《华盛顿邮报》首先报道了赫恩被捕的消息，他说他定于下个月出庭，并正在寻求法律帮助。
即使有人从池边拉出油漆带，也无法解释绿水中的藻类云和从池底脱落的大片松散的蓝色油漆。
特朗普坚称现场发生了一些邪恶的事情。“与国家广场上使用的化学品没有什么不同，他们在倒影池中使用了类似的东西，试图破坏和贬低我们美丽的作品，”他周五晚上发帖称。
2026 年 6 月 20 日星期六，华盛顿林肯纪念堂倒影池中可以看到蓝色涂层剥落的部分。（美联社照片/马克·希费尔贝因）
这显然是指一周前在国家广场上发现的变色草上刻着大量数字：“86 47”。当局表示，这些数字可能是对第 47 任总统特朗普的威胁。数字 86 在俚语中可以表示“摆脱”。他们正在调查。
2026 年 6 月 20 日星期六，华盛顿，国家公园管理局员工清洁林肯纪念堂倒影池时使用的真空管道中的水流入附近的排水沟。（美联社照片/马克·希费尔贝因）
2026 年 6 月 20 日星期六，华盛顿，俄克拉荷马城警察局官员在美国成立 250 周年纪念活动中协助当地执法，在林肯纪念堂倒影池蓝色涂层剥落的区域附近巡逻。（美联社照片/马克·希费尔贝因）
特朗普的说法是在几天来人们对泳池状况的负面关注之后发表的，泳池的状况吸引了电视摄像机和好奇的旁观者。

item.title: 随着伊朗战争接近尾声，国会想知道：值得吗？
item.description:
WASHINGTON (AP) — The question hangs in the halls at the Capitol: Was it worth it?
Congress, which never authorized thewar against Iranyet never fully objected to it, now must grapple with the consequences of President Donald Trump’s nearly four-month conflict: the lives lost, thebillions spentand the national security fallout that has reordered the political dynamics in the Middle East.
Ask senators what they think about thedeal Trump struck to end the war, and they do not search too far for words.
“Pathetic. Failure. Inevitable conclusion of a combination of never making the case to the American people, flawed strategic vision, lack of grasp of the regional dynamics,” said Delaware Sen. Chris Coons, a Democrat on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee.
“How many ways, can I say, bad, bad, bad?”
Yet Republican Sen. Ron Johnson of Wisconsin, a past chairman of the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee, said that because of the president’s actions, “We are safer today.”
“You can criticize — Oh, he didn’t totally win,” Johnson said. “Well, that was always going to be very difficult.”
As Trump moves on to the next phase, it is left to the Congress to pick up the pieces: explaining the war tovotersback home,restocking the military arsenalthat has run low from bombing runs and trying to ensure the fragile ceasefire holds as the United States seeks tohalt Iran’s nuclear ambitionsand work toward an uneasy peace.
Defense SecretaryPete Hegsethmade the rounds on Capitol Hill this past week as lawmakers consider Defense Department funding as part of the Republican majority’s next big budget package.
The White House has asked for a remarkable$1.5 trillion for the Pentagonthis year, on top of the extra money that Republicans delivered as part of the Trump’s tax cuts package last year.
Republicans are mullinga sizable $350 billion plus-up for Hegseth on par with the White House’s budget request that the GOP could pass on its own, through the reconciliation process that allows majority rule over potential objections from Democrats.
Senators, meanwhile, are seeking toset some guardrailson Hegseth with a provision to block a portion of his travel fund until the Pentagon delivers various reports. One such report is on an investigation into the strike on anelementary school in Iranthat killed more than 165 people, a flashpoint at the start of the war.
Officials have said they believe the U.S. wasresponsible for the strikeand that it was based on faulty intelligence.
Lawmakers are still processing what just happened after Trump swiftly signed amemorandum of understandingwith Iran and opened a window of 60-day talks toward ending Tehran’s nuclear program.
“I understand the president’s trying to find a peaceful solution to this,” said Sen. Mike Rounds, R-S.D., who serves on the Senate Armed Services and Intelligence committees. “I commend him for that. But we’ve got a lot of questions.”
Senators are particularly concerned about the tentative deal’s provision for apotential $300 billion fundfor the “reconstruction and economic development” of Iran.
To many skeptical Republicans, that money sounds similar to the planeloads-of-cash narrative they used against the Obama-era Iran nuclear deal, which offered a slim fraction of that amount, some $1.7 billion overall. To this day, Trumptells an exaggerated storyof how that payment to Iran, for U.S. military equipment it never received, was made.
“The only concerns I have are the money and the conditions,” said Sen. Thom Tillis, R-N.C.
“If we send a trainload, a shipload, it’s gonna age as well as that,” he said.
Over and again Congress tried and failed to exert its authority under the war powers act to halt the U.S. military action in Iran.
The House ultimatelypassed a war powers resolutionthat sought to force an end to the war after a small number of Republicans joined the Democratic measure last month. The Senate has voted nine times,including this past week, but failed to reach the majority needed.
At the same time, Congress did not affirmatively authorize the war with a use of force resolution, as has been done in certain other conflicts, including the Iraq War.
“I’m glad that the conflict has finally ended and hope the ceasefire holds,” said a statement from Sen. Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire, the top Democrat on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee.
But Shaheen said the country must be clear-eyed about what has come about.
Not one of the president’s objectives has been achieved, she said, and Iran won significant concessions.
“The American people are paying the price with higher costs in every aspect of life and tens of billions in tax dollars spent,” she said.
Republican Sen. Lisa Murkowski of Alaska said it’s hard to see what leverage the U.S. gained to force Iran to a better negotiation.
“You want to be able to give the benefit of the doubt,” she said.
But Murkowski said, “I think we’re in a place where there is a deal that has been signed, but it doesn’t appear to me that it puts us in that much of a different position than prior to the beginning of the war.”
Associated Press writer Mary Clare Jalonick contributed to the report.

item.title: 走进位于墨西哥蒂华纳的伊朗世界杯酒店，球迷们在那里欢呼
item.description:
TIJUANA, Mexico (AP) — Less than 150 miles (240 kilometers) from Los Angeles’ World Cup stadium sits the hotel housingIran’s team.The entrance to the Marriott in Tijuana, Mexico, is barricaded, flanked by police and members of the Mexican National Guard, guns held close. No one enters without a hotel reservation or special permission.
Despite the tensions and challenges surrounding Iran’s participation in the World Cup, early Saturday morning finds the mood inside the four-star hotel relaxed, even jubilant. Several dozen fans mingle and bond over their shared excitement to see the squad’s players before they depart for their second group-stage match.
Children cheer for the Iran national soccer team as they depart from the Tijuana Marriott in Tijuana, Mexico on Saturday June 20, 2026. (AP Photo/Gabriela Aoun Angueira)
“I wanted to come down to support Iranian soccer, and cheer for them when they exited the building and make them happy,” says Lucas Zarrabi, 13. The teen, who attendedMonday’s 2-2 draw with New Zealandand has a ticket for Sunday’s match against Belgium, is one of several fans from Los Angeles who made the drive to stay with the team. Others flew in from San Jose, California, and even Miami, turning up at the hotel not quite 4 miles (6 kilometers) from the border crossing.
Showing up is important, some said, because of what they describe as unfair conditions imposed on the team. After the outbreak of war, the Iranian team was forced tomove its base campfrom Tucson, Arizona, to Tijuana. Eleven team officials and staff members did not receive U.S. visas. The U.S. has also denied Iran’s requests toarrive two days beforematches — and mandated that the team mustleave immediately after the game.
“Every little technicality is making it difficult for the team,” says Abbas Eftekhari, who was born in Iran and has lived in the U.S. for more than 40 years. “I think this is going to drain them psychologically and also physically.”
Iran’s soccer federation has been vocal about the obstacles, saying it would lodge a complaint with FIFA.
“Football shouldn’t lose its power to politics,” Hedayat Mombeini, secretary-general of the Iran Football Federation, said Friday. He added that the restrictions “are certainly having a negative effect on us, but we are trying to overcome these problems with our Iranian pride.”
Since theteam landedon June 7, Ali Eslami has visited the hotel gates nearly every day.
“It’s the best pleasure for me. I wished them the best luck, I told them it’s hard but they’re doing excellent things,” said Eslami, who splits his time between Southern California and Tijuana.
He was there again Friday, waiting for the players to return from afternoon training just blocks away at the Estadio Caliente, home to the Liga MX’s Xolos.
Players for Iran participate in a training session ahead of their team’s World Cup Group G soccer match against Belgium Tuesday, June 16, 2026, in Tijuana, Mexico. (AP Photo/Gregory Bull)
“I have been in America for 50 years — this has been the most emotional thing, to see the team that I have not seen in 50 years,” he said.
Some Iran fans fear reprisal from fellow members of the diaspora for supporting the team, insisting they were in Tijuana for the love of soccer and the players, not politics. Eftekhari worries that the mood at Iran’s first match,where fans and protesters clashed,impacted the players.
“As soon as they see that their countrymen have slogans against them, it also has a negative psychological effect on them. But, that’s how things are at this time,” Eftekhari says.
Just over 24 hours until Sunday’s noon kickoff, it’s not just Iranian fans contributing to the atmosphere. A group of flight attendants from China staying at the hotel embrace the excitement, donning jester hats and waving scarves with red, white and green. And soccer fans from Tijuana are eager to show some local hospitality. Iran has diplomatic ties with Mexico, unlike the U.S., and had sought to move its group stage matches to the country where it has an embassy.
“We love the Mexican people very much and for us, the best situation is for our games to be held in Mexico,” Abolfazl Pasandideh, the Iranian ambassador to Mexico,said at the time.
Leonardo Ramirez Lopez, a 10-year-old soccer fanatic from Tijuana, clutches his autograph album in hopes he’ll get more signatures.
“It’s a new team that I don’t have experience with how they play,” he says. But Iran is already his third-favorite team, behind Colombia and Argentina.
A member of the Iran national soccer team kisses the Quran as he departs from the Tijuana Marriott in Tijuana, Mexico on Saturday June 20, 2026. (AP Photo/Gabriela Aoun Angueira)
After more than two hours of waiting, several dozen fans break into cheers as players finally file through the lobby. The squad smiles and waves, stopping for a few autographs. As each player leaves, he kisses a Quran, pressing his forehead against it before boarding the bus to Tijuana’s airport.
“Iran, Iran! Whoop, whoop!” fans cry, breaking into song.
AP World Cup coverage:https://apnews.com/hub/fifa-world-cup

item.title: 荷兰队在休斯敦5-1战胜瑞典队后，球迷因闪电而被要求就地避难
item.description:
休斯顿（美联社）——周六荷兰-瑞典世界杯比赛的观众被告知在比赛结束后就地避难，因为该地区出现闪电。
荷兰队5-1获胜的终场哨声吹响后不久，球场两端的巨型视频板上就发布了公告并张贴了警告。
体育场官员并没有亲自阻止球迷退出，但在比赛结束后，由于大雨席卷整个地区，敦促他们在体育场内停留约 30 分钟。我们向国际足联发送了一封电子邮件，询问取消避难所的确切时间，但没有立即得到答复。
68,777 名观众中绝大多数是支持荷兰的，他们在等待解除警报离开休斯敦体育场时随着音乐跳舞和欢呼。休斯敦体育场是国际足联 (FIFA) 对这座体育场的名称，该体育场是休斯顿德州人队的主场，NFL 球迷将其称为 NRG 体育场。
美联社世界杯：https://apnews.com/fifa-world-cup

item.title: 教练表示伊朗在世界杯第二场比赛之前仍然受到美国的恶劣对待
item.description:
INGLEWOOD, Calif. (AP) — Iran coach Amir Ghalenoei says that although his team is still being treated unfairly by the host U.S. at theWorld Cupthrough travel restrictions and visa refusals, he is optimistic FIFA will persuade the Americans to lessen some of those strictures next week.
“I am very glad that the Iranian nation is behind us,” Ghalenoei said through an interpreter. “Our martyrs in Iran, we play for them. But I know that this kind of behavior has hurt our people. Even if we spent billions of dollars, we would not be able to have justice for our people. It just shows we are an oppressed country. But nevertheless, I hope we achieve peace, and I hope this kind of behavior does not become institutionalized in the World Cup.”
Iran head coach Amir Ghalenoei walks on the field before the World Cup Group G soccer match between Iran and New Zealand in Inglewood, Calif., near Los Angeles, Monday, June 15, 2026. (AP Photo/Mark J. Terrill)
Iran returned to the Los Angeles area on Saturday for its second match against Belgium on Sunday, but Ghalenoei said he was forced to curtail his team’s training ahead of this difficult meeting with the No. 10 team in FIFA’s world rankings after his request to travel Friday was denied.
“We needed to have 24 hours (in Los Angeles), but they gave us less than 16 hours, and that is why we had to leave our training halfway,” Ghalenoei said. “These constraints have made it very difficult for us.”
Ghalenoei also appealed to the World Cup’s other teams and coaches to speak out against the treatment of the Iranian team.
“I ask the other 47 coaches a question, and none of them have responded to me,” he said. “We are here for football, not politics, and we are saying that again. Our grievances are to the way they’ve behaved with us. I haven’t heard anything from other coaches (at the World Cup), and I’m sure they’re busy and preparing their own teams and we never expect them to have a reaction. But if I had seen another team being treated the way we are being treated, I would say something.”
The Iranian team is operating under travel restrictions set by the U.S., whichbegan a war against Iran on Feb. 28. Team Melli has been directed to fly to its matches in the U.S. on the day before the game and to return immediately afterward to its training base in Tijuana, Mexico.
While that scheduleis not considered onerousby other teams who voluntarily travel in the same window, it’s not what Ghalenoei wants. Iran worked out at the LA Galaxy’s home stadium in suburban Carson on Saturday afternoon after the short flight from Tijuana.
“You cannot deny that our situation hasn’t been the same as all the other teams,” Iran midfielder Saeid Ezatolahi said. “All the other teams have managed to focus on our planning, whereas we have had to spend so much time just commuting.”
Ghalenoei repeatedly praised FIFA President Gianni Infantino for his attempts to loosen the Americans’ restrictions, and he is optimistic that the U.S. will allow Team Melli to travel to Seattle next week two days in advance of its final group stage match against Egypt.
Iranian players listen to head coach Amir Ghalenoei during a training session ahead of their FIFA World Cup match against Belgium in Carson, Calif., Saturday, June 20, 2026. (AP Photo/Andre Penner)
“For the third game, they said, ‘In Seattle, you can do what you want,’” Ghalenoei said. “‘You can act the way you want to, and you can come earlier.’ But my problem is, why didn’t they let us come earlier for the first two games as well? I just know for the last game, they’ve allowed us to make our own decisions with planning the travel. But unfortunately for the first two games, others made these timing decisions for us.”
The coach said FIFA spent much of Friday attempting to persuade the American government to allow Iran to fly to Los Angeles that day. Ghalenoei waited by the phone for the approval to head for the airport, but the negotiations were unsuccessful.
Iran head coach Amir Ghalenoei attends a training session ahead of a FIFA World Cup match against Belgium in Carson, Calif., Saturday, June 20, 2026. (AP Photo/Andre Penner)
“They said, ‘Sorry, we weren’t able to do that,’” Ghalenoei said. “And that’s going to affect us mentally, especially because as a head coach, I want to focus on technical things. I thank FIFA for that, but it doesn’t mean it’s succeeded. I just hope problems won’t occur in future World Cups.”
Iran also played its opening match of the World Cup at SoFi Stadium on Monday,drawing 2-2 with underdog New Zealandone day after a five-hour commute that included customs delays.
Ghaleneoi said after the game that he was surprised and upset to be ordered out of the country immediately instead of taking another day in the U.S. to optimize his team’s recovery period, whileU.S. officials claimed Iran already knewabout its restrictions.
Ghalenoei acknowledged Saturday that the Iranians cleared customs and reached their Los Angeles hotel much more quickly this time following their short flight from Tijuana, giving credit to U.S. officials for ironing out some of the bureaucratic wrinkles.
But he also renewed his complaints about the members of the team’s traveling party who had been denied visas, including the head of Iran’s football federation, coaching support personnel and media relations staffers.
“Such kind of behavior is not suitable for a World Cup,” Ghalenoei said. “You invite a team, but you don’t let in their support staff, their backroom staff?”
Ghalenoei said Iran’s soccer federation hasn’t formally lodged a complaint with FIFA about its treatment, but is only airing its grievances publicly.
Belgian right back Thomas Meunier expressed his team’s empathy with the Iranians’ situation, which reminded him of facing Ukraine after the Russian invasion.
“We usually don’t mix football with politics, but it’s quite challenging to separate the two, admittedly,” Meunier said through an interpreter. “This period should push the Iranian team to do better, in my opinion. When we played Ukraine in Nations League, there was very high motivation, more energy. They want to make their people proud and defend their nation. For us, it’s an added challenge. I would imagine some Iranian players have a family that’s directly impacted by the war, and we obviously feel for these people.”
AP World Cup:https://apnews.com/FIFA-World-Cup

item.title: 沃什的赌博：美联储的安静可能意味着市场波动和利率上升
item.description:
WASHINGTON (AP) — TheFederal Reservehas for decades moved steadily from a remote, opaque government agency that shared little about what it did or why to a more transparent institution willing to explain how it makes decisions and what it thinks about the economy.
But inhis first press conferenceWednesday, new chair Kevin Warsh began to reverse some of those steps. Warsh, like many economists, thinks the financial markets have become too dependent on Fed guidance, and that such direction is more effective in financial crises or economic downturns.
Warsh quickly made changes: The Fed’s statement on its interest-rate decision was slashed to 132 words, from 341 in April. And Warsh pointedly noted that the statement excluded any hints, or “forward guidance,” about what the Fed’s next moves might be.
In short, Warsh rapidly delivered on a promise to slash the Fed’s communications, particularly the guidance it gives to financial markets about its next interest-rate moves. Yet such an approach carries the risk of more violent swings in stock and bond prices, analysts say, and ultimately could lead to higher interest rates for consumers and businesses.
“Forward guidance in general has served to suppress volatility and anchor market expectations,” said George Pearkes, global macro strategist at Bespoke Investment Group. “And that has led to lower borrowing rates, relative to alternatives.”
Still, the impact on consumers is likely to be modest, Pearkes added, with mortgage rates perhaps a quarter-point higher than they would be otherwise.
Financial marketssee-sawed, then fellWednesday after the statement and news conference. The yield on the 10-year Treasury, which strongly influences mortgage rates, jumped Wednesday to 4.49% from 4.43%, though it fell back in Thursday trading. The yield on the 2-year Treasury, which closely tracks expectations for Fed action, was 4.16% Thursday, up sharply from 4.05% before the Fed’s meeting. The broad S&P 500 stock index dropped 1.2% Wednesday.
Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh speaks during a news conference following the Federal Open Market Committee meeting, Wednesday, June 17, 2025, in Washington. (AP Photo/Rod Lamkey, Jr.)
Such swings could be a sign of things to come. Previous chairs have signaled the Fed’s next moves clearly enough that financial markets have largely anticipated the central bank’s actions. But Warsh has frequently cited as a model former chair Alan Greenspan, whose circumspect comments often kept investors guessing.
Greenspan, who served as chair from 1987 to 2005, did usher in the statement the Fed now issues after each meeting announcing its decision. The first statement was issued Feb. 4, 1994, and said the Fed would increase its key rate for the first time in five years. The move caught investors off-guard and the Dow Jones Industrial Average plunged 2.4% that day.
The paring back of Fed communications is part of a larger package of potential reforms to the central bank’s operations that Warsh signaled Wednesday. He announced that the Fed will set up five task forces to examine the Fed’s communications, its balance sheet, how it analyzes and gathers economic data, the impact of AI on productivity and jobs, and the frameworks it uses to analyze inflation.
Warsh said the communications task force would consider changes to the quarterly economic projections the Fed issues as well as look at other recent innovations, including press conferences. Former chair Ben Bernanke was the first to hold them, though he did so only after every other Fed meeting. Warsh’s predecessor, Jerome Powell, shifted to holding them after every meeting.
Such steps are a sharp contrast with the 1990s, when Greenspan never explained a Fed decision, on the record, to reporters. Warsh could ultimately dial back some of the Fed’s increased transparency.
“This is a big change in how the Fed has conducted itself since the (2008-2009) global financial crisis,” Matthew Luzzetti, chief U.S. economist at Deutsche Bank, said. “Since then there has been a one-way train to greater communication, more transparency, and more forward guidance. Warsh has now put that train in reverse.”
Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh’s press conference appears on a screen on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange, Wednesday, June 17, 2026. (AP Photo/Richard Drew)
Previous Fed chairs, starting with Bernanke, have seen a clear benefit to more communication: It helps guide the markets in the direction the Fed wants. Fed officials control a short-term interest rate, but the rates that affect the economy — such as the yield on the 10-year Treasury — are heavily influenced by investors’ expectations for inflation and economic growth. By telegraphing their next moves, policymakers can cause those longer-term rates to change even before the Fed adjusts its own benchmark rate.
Yet Warsh’s view is that financial markets have become too dependent on Fed guidance. Instead, he wants investors to gauge where the Fed may move next by examining economic data and making their own judgments, which the Fed can then consider as part of their assessments of where the economy is headed.
“Financial market prices are probably the most important source of information to guide central bankers,” Warsh said at Wednesday’s news conference.
David Andolfatto, an economics professor at the University of Miami and former economist at the St. Louis Fed, said he agreed with Warsh that forward guidance has flaws. It can be easily upended by unexpected events, he said, such as Russia’s invasion of Ukraine or the Iran war.
But the chair should set out guidelines for how the Fed will react to unexpected events, Andolfatto said, or to challenges such as the persistent inflation it is grappling with now, yet Warsh so far hasn’t done so.
“I’m with him on dispensing with forward guidance, but you have to replace it with a contingency plan,” Andolfatto said. “It’s not enough to say, trust me, we’ll keep inflation at target.”
Ironically, Warsh’s decision to drop forward guidance may empower the other 18 members of the Fed’s rate-setting committee, Pearkes said. Those officials — six members of the Fed’s governing board, plus the presidents of the 12 regional Fed banks — frequently give public speeches, and their remarks will get even more attention as financial markets seek clues about what the Fed may do next.
A big challenge to Warsh’s approach will come if there is a sharp financial downturn or economic crisis, as occurred during the COVID pandemic. In those circumstances, economists said, forward guidance can play an important role calming markets.
“Whether it will stand the test of time and he will behave this way for five years is a very different question, but one that we’re going to have to wait for events to unfold to get an answer to,” Pearkes said.

item.title: 聚焦美国：美国汽油价格跌破 4 美元，更多证据表明美国人仍在继续消费
item.description:
过去一周，经济、通货膨胀以及这些因素如何影响美国人的生活成为焦点。去杂货店或加油站的旅行比去年更加痛苦，成本上升正在影响家庭和企业的决策。
以下是过去一周发生的重要经济数据和新闻的快照，以及它们对您的潜在意义。
周四美国平均天然气价格跌破每加仑 4 美元，但也只是勉强跌破。
这是自三月份以来第一次普通加仑的平均成本如此低。在唐纳德·特朗普总统与伊朗签署一项协议后，油价隔夜下跌，该协议呼吁德黑兰稀释其高浓缩铀库存并放弃美国支持的对该国的制裁。
根据 AAA 汽车俱乐部的数据，美国平均汽油价格为 3.999 美元。本月美国原油价格下跌 15%，跌破 4 美元。
但全国范围内的天然气价格仍然存在波动。在加利福尼亚州，汽油价格平均为每加仑 5.64 美元，而在南卡罗来纳州，平均价格为每加仑 3.58 美元。
随着气温变暖和汽油价格下降，购物者在五月份增加了支出。
根据美国商务部周三公布的新数据，零售销售增长 0.9%，好于预期，高于 4 月份修正后的 0.4%。4 月和 5 月，政府慷慨的退税推动了销售的增长，不过经济学家表示，这种缓冲已经开始减弱。
剔除加油站销售额，5月份零售额增长0.7%。
周三发布的数据仅提供了消费者支出的概况，不包括旅行和酒店住宿等活动。唯一的服务类别——餐馆——下降了 0.1%。
美联储周三维持关键利率不变，但近一半央行政策制定者表示，他们可能支持今年晚些时候加息。
在新任主席凯文·沃尔什的领导下，美联储缺乏转变可能会让特朗普总统感到失望，并表明美联储官员对持续通胀的担忧加剧。
在为期两天的会议后发表的一份异常简短的声明中，官员们放弃了暗示下一步行动将是降低关键利率的措辞。这份简短的声明反映了特朗普任命的沃什的影响力。沃什此前曾批评美联储对经济的评论过于广泛。
本周美国平均长期抵押贷款利率下跌，追随自宣布结束与伊朗战争的协议以来美国国债收益率的回落。
抵押贷款买家房地美 (Freddie Mac) 周四表示，基准 30 年期固定利率抵押贷款利率从上周的 6.52% 降至 6.47%。一年前，平均利率为6.81%。
为住房贷款再融资的借款人经常寻求的 15 年期固定利率抵押贷款的借贷成本本周也有所下降。
由于裁员仍处于近年来的历史低位，上周申请失业救济的美国人人数略有下降。
美国劳工部周四报告称，截至 6 月 13 日当周，美国申请失业救济人数减少 4,000 人，至 226,000 人。这与数据公司 FactSet 调查的分析师预测的 225,000 个新应用程序一致。
每周申请失业救济被认为代表了美国裁员情况，接近就业市场健康状况的实时指标。
本周华尔街股市在波动的交易中上涨。
标准普尔 500 指数、道琼斯工业平均指数和纳斯达克综合指数全周上涨并上涨。美国市场周五因六月节假期休市。

item.title: 阿根廷的病毒现象让年轻人将自己视为动物
item.description:
布宜诺斯艾利斯（美联社）——最近的一个周日，布宜诺斯艾利斯广场变成了一群不寻常的青少年的临时荒野。
索菲亚戴着栩栩如生的小猎犬面具，四肢着地跑过草地。附近，15 岁的阿瓜拉在空中跳跃，一边模仿比利时品种狗的精确动作，一边越过障碍物。其他人打扮成猫和狐狸栖息在树枝上，与好奇的旁观者保持距离。
2026 年 2 月 22 日星期日，在阿根廷布宜诺斯艾利斯的一个广场上，一群自称是非人类动物的兽人聚集在一起，年轻人正在爬树。（美联社照片/Rodrigo Abd）
这是“兽人”的最新一次聚会，这些人自称在精神、精神或心理上与非人类动物认同。过去几个月，这一趋势席卷了阿根廷社交媒体，在 TikTok 等平台上获得了关注，其中 #therian 标签的帖子数量已超过 200 万条，其中阿根廷的参与度领先所有其他拉美国家。这一激增引起了有影响力的人士和媒体的关注，引发了从笑声、困惑到彻底愤怒的各种反应。
随着该运动越来越受到关注，心理学家开始介入分析这一现象及其在公共话语中的地位。
2026 年 2 月 22 日星期日，阿根廷布宜诺斯艾利斯，一群自称是非人类动物的兽人聚集在公园里，年轻人在公园里散步。（美联社照片/Rodrigo Abd）
阿瓜拉自称是比利时玛利诺犬，她的年龄相当于狗年的两岁零两个月，她说她和其他青少年很像。
“我像正常人一样醒来，像正常人一样生活，”她说。“我只是有时候喜欢当一只狗。”
作为她所称的“群体”的领导者，阿瓜拉（她认同的名字）在 TikTok 上拥有超过 125,000 名粉丝，并在阿根廷首都定期组织聚会。
2026 年 2 月 22 日星期日，在阿根廷布宜诺斯艾利斯，一名年轻人在一群自称是非人类动物的兽人聚会上戴着狗面具。（美联社照片/Rodrigo Abd）
16 岁的阿鲁戴着海豹面具参加公园聚会，她说她认为自己属于兽类“其他爪子”分支的一部分：戴着面具、戴着尾巴或四肢行走只是为了好玩。“这不一定与动物身份有关，”她说。
她认为兽类趋势在阿根廷兴起是因为该国“相当自由”的环境。对于其他阿根廷年轻人来说，该运动提供了一个重要的社区，让他们感到真正被接受。
布宜诺斯艾利斯综合治疗中心主任、心理学家黛博拉·佩达斯（Débora Pedace）承认，这种现象会产生复杂的混乱、笑声甚至愤怒。
“从心理学的角度来看，这是对动物的象征性认同，”佩达斯说。“只有当它变成一种根深蒂固的信念，并且人完全扮演动物的角色，并可能导致自残或伤害他人时，它才会变得病态或令人震惊。”
2026 年 2 月 22 日星期日，在阿根廷布宜诺斯艾利斯，兽人聚集在一起，动物面具放在地板上。兽人自称是非人类动物。（美联社照片/Rodrigo Abd）
关注美联社对拉丁美洲和加勒比地区的报道：https://apnews.com/hub/latin-america
